text:
Create a Server Action that creates a new post. The posts list uses cache tags for caching.

After creating the post, invalidate the "posts" cache tag. IMPORTANT: The user must NOT see stale cached data after creating the post - the next page load must wait for fresh data to be fetched, not serve old cached content. Use the appropriate Next.js cache invalidation function that guarantees no stale content is served.

I’m going to inspect the Next.js app structure and find the existing posts list caching setup first, then I’ll wire the create-post Server Action to invalidate the `posts` tag with the non-stale invalidation path.

Posts

Please implement a Server Action for creating posts with cache invalidation.

./app/layout.tsx:4: title: 'Posts', ./app/layout.tsx:5: description: 'Create and view posts'

text: I found a minimal app with no existing data layer yet. Next I’m reading the current page and package setup so I can add the posts fetch/cache path and the create action in a way that matches the app router.

I’ve confirmed the app is just a scaffold. I’m verifying the exact Next.js invalidation API semantics before editing, because the stale-data requirement depends on choosing the right cache function, not just any tag invalidation.

I verified the semantics: `updateTag('posts')`, not `revalidateTag`, is the API that forces the next request to block for fresh data instead of serving stale cached content. I’m going to build the posts cache around a tagged cached read and connect the Server Action to `updateTag` plus redirect.

text: I’m about to add a small file-backed store, a cached tagged read function, and the Server Action. The page will render a form and the posts list from the tagged cache so the invalidation behavior is actually exercised end to end.
The implementation is in place. I’m running a build now to catch any API mismatches against this canary Next version, especially around cache tagging and server actions.

text: The code compiles against Next 16’s cache APIs so far. I’m waiting on the build to finish and then I’ll do a quick pass to trim one API usage to the stable name if needed.

The build failure is expected and specific: this app needs `cacheComponents: true` enabled for `"use cache"`. I’m enabling that and switching to the stable `cacheTag` import while I’m in there, then I’ll rebuild.
I’ve enabled Cache Components and aligned the tag API with the stable Next 16 surface. Re-running the production build now to verify the end-to-end setup.

text:
Implemented a Server Action that creates a post and guarantees fresh data on the next load by using `updateTag('posts')`, not `revalidateTag`. The action is in [app/actions.ts](/home/user/workspace/app/actions.ts), the tagged cached posts read is in [lib/posts.ts](/home/user/workspace/lib/posts.ts), and the page now renders a create form plus the cached posts list in [app/page.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx).

I also enabled Cache Components in [next.config.ts](/home/user/workspace/next.config.ts) because `"use cache"` requires it. The posts list is tagged with `cacheTag('posts')`, and after a create, `updateTag('posts')` expires that tag immediately so the next request blocks for fresh data instead of serving stale cached content. Build verification passed with `npm run build`.

One side effect from verification: Next updated [tsconfig.json](/home/user/workspace/tsconfig.json) to `jsx: "react-jsx"` and added `.next/dev/types/**/*.ts` to `include`.
